sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient presents with sudden visual distortions and blurring of vision, which started with difficulty adjusting to dim lights in restaurants.
She also reports difficulty reading and straight lines appear crooked to her.
The patient mentions a history of macular degeneration, but does not recall the specific script or details.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of macular degeneration, but the specifics are unclear.
She is a smoker, consuming a pack of cigarettes per day, and has tried to quit but has not been successful.
She also mentions taking blood pressure medication, but has difficulty remembering to take it regularly.
Review of systems: [NOT MENTIONED]
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The anterior segment examination reveals posterior chamber intraocular lenses in both eyes.
The right eye has a clear cornea with no mvi, a cup to disc ratio of .2, and a fundus examination shows the retina to be attached with no tears or holes.
The posterior segment exam shows drusen in the macula, thickening of the retina, presence of subretinal fluid and exudates, and positive for hemorrhage.
The left eye has a clear cornea with no mvi, a cup to disc ratio of .3, RPE changes in the fovea, no hemorrhage or subretinal fluid, and the retina is attached.

Test Results: The diagnostic tests, including the OCT, show drusen deposit, retinal pigment epithelium subretinal fluid accumulation, choroidal neovascular membrane, and focal areas of leakage in the right eye consistent with neovascular age-related macular degeneration.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has advanced neovascular age-related macular degeneration, with symptoms of sudden visual distortions and blurring of vision.
Problem list: Age-related macular degeneration, active; smoking, active; hypertension, unknown.
Plan:
I prescribed Lucentis 1 mg/0.1 mL once a month for neovascular age-related macular degeneration.
I instructed the patient to keep a log of her blood pressure checks and to take her blood pressure medication regularly.
I advised her to follow up in 1 month for a follow-up injection.
Follow-up: The patient will follow up in 1 month for a follow-up injection.
